coredata.dc:title: Activation of microbubbles by low-intensity pulsed ultrasound enhances the cytotoxicity of curcumin involving apoptosis induction and cell motility inhibition in human breast cancer MDA-MB-231 cells
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yixiang
Wang, Pan
Chen, Xiyang
Hu, Jianmin
Liu, Yichen
Wang, Xiaobing
Liu, Quanhong
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasound and microbubbles-mediated drug delivery has become a promising strategy to promote drug delivery and its therapeutic efficacy. The aim of this research was to assess the effects of microbubbles (MBs)-combined low-intensity pulsed ultrasound (LPUS) on the delivery and cytotoxicity of curcumin (Cur) to human breast cancer MDA-MB-231 cells. Under the experimental condition, MBs raised the level of acoustic cavitation and enhanced plasma membrane permeability; and cellular uptake of Cur was notably improved by LPUS–MBs treatment, aggravating Cur-induced MDA-MB-231 cells death. The combined treatment markedly caused more obvious changes of cell morphology, F-actin cytoskeleton damage and cell migration inhibition. Our results demonstrated that combination of MBs and LPUS may be an efficient strategy for improving anti-tumor effect of Cur, suggesting a potential effective method for antineoplastic therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound
Microbubbles
Curcumin
Breast cancer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ultrasonic exposure system and treatment protocols
Flow cytometry analysis of cell apoptosis
Flow cytometry analysis of cell membrane permeability
Enhanced cytotoxicity of Cur combined with MBs-Us treatment
LPUS–MBs enhanced membrane permeability of MDA-MB-231 cells

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram of ultrasound setup. A planar ultrasound transducer is in diameter of 35mm. Space between transducer and cell culture plate was filled with ultrasound couplant. 35mm culture dishes containing cells suspension was exposed above the transducer for 90s one by one with the frequency of 840kHz and the intensity of 1.0W/cm2.

Enhanced cytotoxicity of Cur by LPUS with/without MBs in MDA-MB-231 cells. Cytotoxic effects were determined by MTT assay at 24h post-treatment. Cur: Cur administration at 0, 1, 5 and 10μM respectively; Cur–LPUS: the combined treatment with Cur and LPUS; Cur–LPUS–MBs: the combined treatment with Cur administration and MBs-assisted LPUS exposure. Data are means±S.D. of three independent experiments. * p <0.05 and ** p <0.01, compared with Cur groups at the same concentration of Cur. # p <0.05 and ## p <0.01 compared with Cur–LPUS group at the same concentration of Cur.

Representative AFM and optical microscopy images of MDA-MB-231 cells at 24h after different treatments. The first column (A1–F6) and second column (A2–F2) showed two-dimensional topographic images and three-dimensional height images of MDA-MB-231 cells, while the third column (A3–F3) presented the optical microscopy images. A1, A2 and A3: control group; B1, B2 and B3: Cur group; C1, C2 and C3: LPUS group; D1, D2 and D3: LPUS–MBs group; E1, E2 and E3: Cur–LPUS group; F1, F2 and F3: Cur–LPUS–MBs group.

The average changes in length, breadth and height of the cells after different treatment. Control: untreated group; Cur: 10μM Cur alone treatment; LPUS: LPUS alone treatment; LPUS–MBs: LPUS combined with MBs treatment; Cur–LPUS: 10μM Cur combined with LPUS treatment; Cur–LPUS–MBs: combined treatment of 10μM Cur administration and LPUS–MBs exposure. Data are means±S.D. of three independent experiments obtained through Nanoscope analysis software 1.5. * p <0.05 and ** p <0.01, compared with untreated control.

Apoptosis assessment in MDA-MB-231 cells after different treatment. Cells were stained with Annexin V/7-AAD to analyze the apoptotic cell populations (A). Control: untreated group; Cur: 10μM Cur alone treatment; LPUS: LPUS alone treatment; LPUS–MBs: LPUS combined with MBs treatment; Cur–LPUS: 10μM Cur combined with LPUS treatment; Cur–LPUS–MBs: combined treatment of 10μM Cur administration and LPUS–MBs exposure. Data in section (B) are means±S.D. of three independent experiments.

Changes of cell migration of MDA-MB-231 cells. Inhibitory migration was analyzed using Transwell assay at 24h after different treatment (A). Control: untreated group; Cur: 10μM Cur alone treatment; LPUS: LPUS alone treatment; LPUS–MBs: LPUS combined with MBs treatment; Cur–LPUS: 10μM Cur combined with LPUS treatment; Cur–LPUS–MBs: combined treatment of 10μM Cur administration and LPUS–MBs exposure. Data in section (B) are means±S.D. of three independent experiments. * p <0.05 and ** p <0.01, compared with untreated control; ## p <0.01 compared with Cur–LPUS group.

Changes in the actin cytoskeleton of MDA-MB-231 cells after different treatment. Cells were stained with FITC-phalloidin (green) for F-actin, and DAPI for locating the nuclei. Scale bars, 10μm. (a) Control: untreated group; (b) Cur: 10μM Cur alone treatment; (c) LPUS: LPUS alone treatment; (d) LPUS–MBs: MBs-assisted LPUS treatment; (e) Cur–LPUS: 10μM Cur combined with LPUS treatment; (f) Cur–LPUS–MBs: combined treatment of 10μM Cur administration and MBs-assisted LPUS exposure. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Evaluation of ultrasonic cavitation. TA dosimetry was performed immediately after treatments. The fluorescence emission spectrum of HTA (350–500nm) was scanned with setting the excitation wavelength at 310nm. Control: untreated group; LPUS: LPUS alone treatment; LPUS–MBs: MBs-assisted LPUS treatment. Data are means of three independent experiments.

Detection of the changes of cell membrane permeability in MDA-MB-231 cells. Cells were immediately stained with PI after treatments and then subjected to flow cytometry. The portion of cells with high fluorescent intensity of PI and the mean of fluorescence intensity of PI in different groups were both shown. Control: untreated group; LPUS: LPUS alone treatment; LPUS–MBs: MBs-assisted LPUS treatment. Data are means±SD of three independent experiments. In the histogram, * p <0.05 and ** p <0.01, compared with untreated control; ## p <0.01 compared with LPUS group.

Quantification of Cur-uptake in MDA-MB-231 cells using flow cytometry. Immediately following LPUS exposure with/without MBs, the Cur fluorescence-positive cells were measured by flow cytometry. Control: untreated group; Cur: Cur alone treatment; Cur–LPUS: combination of Cur and LPUS treatment; Cur–LPUS–MBs: combination of Cur and MBs-assisted LPUS. Data are means±S.D. of three independent experiments. ** p <0.01, compared with untreated control; ## p <0.01, compared with free curcumin group; Δp <0.05, compared with Cur–LPUS group.

Quantification of Cur-uptake in MDA-MB-231 cells through fluorescence spectral study. (A) inset showed fluorescence spectra of Cur. The linear plot for the variation of Cur (0–20μM) was made as shown in section (A). In section (B), the content of intracellular Cur was measured after sonication. Control: untreated group; Cur: Cur alone treatment; Cur–LPUS: combination of Cur and LPUS treatment; Cur–LPUS–MBs: combination of Cur and MBs-assisted LPUS. Data are means±S.D. of three independent experiments. ** p <0.01, compared with untreated control; ## p <0.01, compared with free curcumin group; Δp <0.05, compared with Cur–LPUS group.

head.ce:title: Activation of microbubbles by low-intensity pulsed ultrasound enhances the cytotoxicity of curcumin involving apoptosis induction and cell motility inhibition in human breast cancer MDA-MB-231 cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Medicinal Resources and Natural Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Ministry of Education, National Engineering Laboratory for Resource Developing of Endangered Chinese Crude Drugs in Northwest of China, College of Life Sciences, Shaanxi Normal University, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Medicinal Resources and Natural Pharmaceutical Chemistry
National Engineering Laboratory for Resource Developing of Endangered Chinese Crude Drugs in Northwest of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Medicinal Resources and Natural Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Ministry of Education, National Engineering Laboratory for Resource Developing of Endangered Chinese Crude Drugs in Northwest of China, College of Life Sciences, Shaanxi Normal University, Xi’an 710119, Shaanxi, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Medicinal Resources and Natural Pharmaceutical Chemistry
National Engineering Laboratory for Resource Developing of Endangered Chinese Crude Drugs in Northwest of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasound and microbubbles-mediated drug delivery has become a promising strategy to promote drug delivery and its therapeutic efficacy. The aim of this research was to assess the effects of microbubbles (MBs)-combined low-intensity pulsed ultrasound (LPUS) on the delivery and cytotoxicity of curcumin (Cur) to human breast cancer MDA-MB-231 cells. Under the experimental condition, MBs raised the level of acoustic cavitation and enhanced plasma membrane permeability; and cellular uptake of Cur was notably improved by LPUS–MBs treatment, aggravating Cur-induced MDA-MB-231 cells death. The combined treatment markedly caused more obvious changes of cell morphology, F-actin cytoskeleton damage and cell migration inhibition. Our results demonstrated that combination of MBs and LPUS may be an efficient strategy for improving anti-tumor effect of Cur, suggesting a potential effective method for antineoplastic therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: The incidence of breast cancer, the most common cancer in women and the second cause of cancer death in women worldwide, is currently growing [1]. There are three major therapeutic approaches used to treat or control breast cancer: surgery, radiotherapy and chemotherapy. However, breast cancer treatment is still challenging, as those treatments have inevitable effects [1–2]. Since ultrasound (US) is non-invasive and perceived as safe, with relatively low cost, the technique is widely used for the imaging of soft tissues and in certain preclinical/clinical therapeutic studies. To date, ultrasound has been used in a variety of therapeutic applications: together with sensitizing molecules it has been used to affect cancer cells (sonodynamic therapy); it has enhanced the activity of chemotherapeutic molecules in cancer therapy (ultrasound-mediated chemotherapy). All of these strategies use ultrasound, consistently to produce bio-effects that resulted in the death of cancer cells [3]. For breast cancer, the benefits of US therapy include the following: no bleeding, preserving the structure and function of breast tissue, no scarring, and little change to breast shape [4].

US has been considered as a potential strategy that could efficiently enhance the anti-tumor effectiveness of some chemotherapeutics including Doxorubicin, Cycloplatin, Docetaxel, Methotrexate, Diaziquone, Bleomycin, and Cisplatin, both in vitro and in vivo [5–7]. The potential mechanism of ultrasound to increase the intracellular uptake of therapeutic agents in cancer cells has been investigated. It is confirmed that US can increase permeability of living membrane and to disrupt cell layers depending on numerous in vitro and in vivo experiments [8]. The acoustic cavitation is responsible for plasma membrane permeabilization, which has been ascribed to pores in the membrane of US-treated cells and was therefore termed as sonoporation. Sonoporation induced by US has been shown to be an effective method to improve drug uptake, and work to promote the delivery of therapeutic agents into lesion location. Importantly, the efficacy of sonoporation is influenced by a variety of factors, such as tissue type, drug properties and US parameters. Yu et al. confirmed the synergistic effect of Adriamycin on resistant ovarian cancers combined with ultrasound exposure with the frequency of 0.24MHz, the intensity of 7.84W/cm2 with exposure duration of 10min in continuous waves [9]. Bekeredjian et al. reported that US with optimization conditions (1.3–12MHz) significantly increased delivery of viral and plasmid transgenes into rat myocardium in vivo [10]. The optimization of ultrasonic parameters and exact mechanism of US-mediated drug delivery remains to be further explored. It is clear that the generation of sonoporation facilitates the cellular uptake of exogenous molecules and results in the subsequent biological responses meanwhile it do not damage the surrounding normal tissues [11–12].

Microbubbles (MBs), initially developed as ultrasonic contrast agents for diagnostic imaging are now widely applied in therapeutic applications MBs are small gas-filled microspheres ranging from 1 to 10μm in diameter. MBs provide nuclei for the initiation of cavitation. The expansion, contraction, and violent collapse of MBs in response to US can create microstreaming, high velocity jets and shock waves. Such events can be used to move macromolecules towards targeted tissue/cells [13–15]. The application of MBs and US in improving drug delivery to tumors is ideal because the ultrasound pressures required are modest and clinically applicable and the inertial cavitation events created are targetable and produce distinct therapeutic effect.

Curcumin (Cur), an active component of the spice turmeric (Curcuma longa), has therapeutic potential: anti-oxidant, anti-inflammatory, antiviral and so on [16–18]. Studies have pointed out that antineoplastic feasibility of Cur because it is preferentially taken up by tumor cells compared with normal cells, and exhibits remarkable inhibitory effects on tumor cells [19]. Several clinical researches on the anti-tumor efficacy of Cur have been also being cited [20]. However, due to low bioavailability and high metabolic clearance rate, curcumin exhibits desirable pharmacological effects only at high dose [21]. Since US and MB-targeted drug delivery (UMTD) has been proposed as an innovative method for non-invasive and tissue-specific delivery of bioactive agents into tumor cells, it may potentially serve to lower the required dosage amounts of Cur, enhance its cellular uptake and antineoplastic effects.

From these above perspective, the aim of this study was to utilize Cur and a clinical MBs, SonoVue® combined with low-intensity pulsed US (LPUS), to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the co-administration on human breast cancer MDA-MB-231 cells. Under MBs-combined LPUS treatment, ultrasonic cavitation effects, plasma membrane permeabilization and Cur-uptake would be analyzed. Simultaneously, the changes of cell morphology, motility and the F-actin cytoskeleton would be observed carefully. The findings may shed some light on the combined antineoplastic therapy of Cur and US.

SonoVue® (Bracco, Milan, Italy) was supplied as a sterile lyophilized powder (25mg) in a 10ml vial filled with gaseous atmosphere (SF6). The SonoVue® MBs suspension was prepared in-situ by mixing with 5ml of sterile saline according to the manufacturer’s instructions and directly applied for the present study without any modification.

Cur was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA), kept as a stock solution of 200mM in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and stored at −20°C. 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) and propidium iodide (PI) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. FITC-phalloidine was supplied by Molecular Probes Inc. (Carlsbad, CA, USA). Guava Nexin reagent was obtained from Guava Technologies (Hayward, CA, USA). Terephthalic acid (TA) was purchased from Alfa Aesar Company (Ward Hill, MA, USA).
All the other reagents were commercial products of analytical grade.

Human breast cancer MDA-MB-231 cells were obtained from the cell bank of the Chinese Academy of Science, Shanghai, China. The cell line was cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM, Gibco, Life Technologies, Grand Island, NY, USA) supplemented with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (Life Technologies, Carlsbad, CA, USA), 100U/ml penicillin–streptomycin solution (Hyclone, Logan, UT, USA) and 1mM l-glutamine (Sigma–Aldrich) at 37°C in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2. Cells in the exponential growth phase with a viability of ⩾98% determined by trypan blue exclusion test were used for this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasonic exposure system and treatment protocols
ce:section.ce:para: Therapeutic pulsed ultrasound apparatus (model: 838A-H-L-S), manufactured by Sheng Xiang High Technology co., LTD (Shenzhen, China), was applied in this study (Fig. 1 ). The application parameters were: frequency of 840kHz, intensity of 1.0W/cm2, duration of 90s, diameter of its planar transducer of 35mm. The transducer worked at an intermittent mode of 5s on and 2s off. For US irradiation, the interval between transducer and cell culture plate was filled with acoustic couplant to mediate ultrasonic transmission. During the process of ultrasonic treatment, temperature increase inside the culture plates was measured before and after ultrasound treatment with a digital thermometer, and no significant variation of temperature was detected (±1°C). Thus, any bio-effects observed in this study were considered to be non-thermal.

MDA-MB-231 cells in the exponential phase were collected, suspended in a complete culture medium at cell densities of 2×105 cells/ml and cultured in 35mm cell culture dish (Corning Inc. Tewksbury MA, USA) for 12h. Then all samples were randomly divided into different groups. Six replicates were in each group. Cur was diluted with culture medium to different concentrations as specified. The diluted solution of SonoVue® (the final concentration of MBs was 2×106 MBs/ml) were added into each dish successively just before US application. After treatment, cells were incubated for an additional time as specified and subjected to subsequent analyses. In the present study we did not include either MBs alone or Cur plus MBs treatment groups as MBs on its own did not contribute to the results of subsequent tests [22].

In order to monitor the cytotoxicity of different treatment, cell viability was detected by MTT assay as previous description [23]. Briefly, at 24h after treatment, cells were harvested and 100μl of the cell suspension in each group was added to 96-well culture plates; the viability was determined by adding 10μl MTT solution (5mg/ml in phosphate buffered saline, PBS) to each well, and incubated for additional 4h at 37°C in a CO2 incubator. The formazan crystals formed were dissolved in pure DMSO, and the absorbance at 570nm was recorded using an ELx800 Microplate Absorbance Reader (Bio-Tek, Winooski, VT, USA). The cell viability of treated cell samples was then obtained by comparing to the incubated but non-exposed control.

Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) and optical microscopy are useful to observe the cells damaged. At 24h after treatment, cells were washed and fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde at room temperature for 10min, then imaged with nitride coated silicon tip in ScanAsyst-fluid mode, which is an imaging mode with automatic image optimization technology by BioScope Catalyst (Bruker, German). Nanoscope analysis software 1.5 was used for image processing and data analysis. Moreover, the morphological changes of cells were also captured under the optical microscope (Nikon, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flow cytometry analysis of cell apoptosis
ce:section.ce:para: Quantification of cell apoptosis was performed at 24h post treatment by a Guava Nexin assay that utilizes Annexin V-PE to detect the externalization of phosphatidylserine on the external membrane of apoptotic cells; the membrane impermeant dye, 7-amino-actinomycin D (7-AAD), is used as an indicator of cell membrane integrity [20]. Briefly, 100μl cells of each sample were suspended in a mixture of 100μl Annexin V-PE and 7-AAD working buffer. After incubation at room temperature for 20min, samples were detected by flow cytometer (Guava easyCyte 8HT, Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA) and analyzed using FCS Express software (De Novo Software, Los Angeles, CA, USA).

The changes of cell motility were evaluated by transwell migration assay. A modified Boyden chamber consisting of a transwell chamber plate with 8-μm pore size polycarbonate membrane filters (Corning Pharmingen, San Diego, CA) was used. After different treatment, cells were collected and suspended in serum-free DMEM medium, then planted to the upper chamber, whereas the bottom chamber was filled with DMEM medium containing 10% serum. After incubation for 24h, the cells remaining on the upper side of the membrane were cleared; and the cells on the underside of membrane were fixed with methanol and stained with 0.1% crystal violet solution. Stained Cells were observed by optical microscopy. Then the crystal violet adhered in the stained cells were dissolved in 30% acetic acid, the absorbance at 570nm was recorded using an ELx800 Microplate Absorbance Reader. The cell migration level of treated samples was obtained by comparing to the incubated but non-exposed control.

Fluorescence staining and laser confocal scanning microscopic observation were performed to evaluate the changes of F-actin cytoskeleton. At 24h after different treatments, cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10min. Cells were permeabilized with 0.1% Triton X-100 for 5min and then blocked with 1% BSA for 1h. Subsequently, cells were stained with 5mg/ml FITC-phalloidine for 1h in the dark. DAPI (4μg/ml) was used to label cell nucleus. Images were obtained by a laser scanning confocal microscope (TCS SP5, Leica, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flow cytometry analysis of cell membrane permeability
ce:section.ce:para: Uptake of PI by cells exposed to external stimuli is a common method for revealing cell membrane permeabilization, as this dye is essentially non-fluorescent in the extracellular medium, but the emission increases upon entering cells and binding to nucleic acids [24]. Immediately after US exposure, cells were rinsed thrice with PBS, stained with 0.01mg/ml PI for 5min in the dark and then detected fluorescent intensity of intracellular PI by flow cytometry. Data were analyzed using FCS Express V3.

Terephthalate dosimetry gives a direct sonochemical efficiency that is sensitive to hydroxyl (
OH) radicals. Non-fluorescent terephthalic acid (TA) reacts with
OH and transfers to fluorescent 2-hysroxyterephthalate ions (HTA), which can be applied as acoustic cavitation level instructions. As we previously mentioned [25], the prepared TA solution (1×10−4 M) was subjected to US treatment with/without MBs in darkness. Immediately after sonication, the fluorescence emission spectrum of HTA (350–500nm) was scanned by fluorescence spectrophotometer (LS-55, PerkinElmer Company, USA) with setting the excitation wavelength at 310nm.

Uptake of Cur by MDA-MB-231 cells was evaluated through detecting its spontaneous fluorescence. Flow cytometry could be used to detect the Cur-uptake in MDA-MB-231 cells without additional markers. After different treatments, cells were immediately washed with PBS thrice. The Cur fluorescent-positive cells were then measured by flow cytometry. Simultaneously, fluorescence spectral study was also performed to measure the cellular content of Cur as previous description [19]. Firstly, the fluorescent standard curve of Cur in methanol solution was made. At 30min after treatments, cells were suspended in methanol and then well sonicated. After centrifuged, the fluorescence intensity of Cur in the supernatant was tested by fluorescence spectrophotometer. The protein content of each group was measured using the BCA protein assay at the same time. Cur uptake level was presented as the content of Cur relative to the total protein content.

Data were presented as means±standard deviation (S.D.) obtained from three independent experiments. Differences among the groups were assessed via one-way analysis of the variance (one-way ANOVA) and p <0.05 was considered statistically significant, while p <0.01 was highly significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced cytotoxicity of Cur combined with MBs-Us treatment
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 , Cur alone (1–10μM) inhibited cell viability in a dose-dependent manner. When cells were exposed to Cur–LPUS–MBs treatment, the viability rate remarkably decreased compared with relative Cur alone group. The viability rate of Cur–LPUS–MBs group was even only 43.64% in the present of 10μM Cur. Cur combined with LPUS caused the cell survival rate fell to 67.25% while the Cur, LPUS or LPUS–MBs just made the viability inhibition less than 25% (the cell viability rate was 86.73% in Cur alone group; 91.47% in LPUS alone group; and 79.39% in LPUS–MBs group). These results indicated that Cur treatment combined with MBs-assisted LPUS induced a significant increment of cell death on MDA-MB-231 cells. Additionally, the 10μM Cur was chosen as the identical dosage for the subsequent measurements.

As shown in Fig. 3 , the first column (A1–F1) and second column (A2–F2) are two-dimensional topographic images and three-dimensional height images of MDA-MB-231 cells captured by AFM at 24h after treatments. In the control group (A1 and A2), cells revealed typical long spindle shaped morphology and obvious pseudopodium connecting each other. The cell membranes were relatively intact and granular. With the treatment of Cur (B1 and B2), slight changes in cells morphology were noted as certain smooth with intact morphology. Both in LPUS (C1 and C2) and LPUS–MBs (D1 and D2) groups, cells presented a certain shrinkage but relative regular shape. After treated by Cur–LPUS (E1 and E2), obvious morphology changes were observed, such as shrinkage and irregular shape. Notably, cells treated by Cur–LPUS–MBs (F1 and F2) clearly showed serious damage, more severe shrinkage and roundout, even collapse. Cell surface granules and pseudopodium disappear. The average changes occurring in length, breadth and height of the cells of the each group were also analyzed through Nanoscope analysis software 1.5 and shown in Fig. 4 . The images captured under optical microscope shown in the third column (A3–F3) also verified that the treatment of Cur–LPUS–MBs caused the most serious damage on MDA-MB-231 cells.

Flow cytometry with Annexin V-PE/7-AAD staining was performed to estimate cell apoptosis following various treatments. As shown in Fig. 5 (A), compared with control, the fractions of apoptotic cells (in both lower-right and upper-right quadrants) in Cur groups were 6.84%, while LPUS and LPUS–MBs caused 12.73% and 20.96% respectively. Cur–LPUS caused higher apoptosis rate in MDA-MB-231 cells (29.9% in the fraction of apoptotic cells). But obviously, after treated by Cur–LPUS–MBs, the proportions of apoptotic cells were up to 39.70%. It was clear in Fig. 5(B) that compared with the control or other groups, Cur–LPUS–MBs treatment markedly increased the percentage of apoptotic cells.

Transwell migration assay have been widely used for studying the motility of different types of cells including metastatic cancer cells. As shown in Fig. 6 (A), numerous cells in control and Cur alone groups moved to the underside of the chamber membrane at 24h post treatment; LPUS and LPUS–MBs could partially prevented cells to migrate. It was the most remarkable that Cur–LPUS–MBs significantly retarded cell movement across the transwell membrane. Relative cell migration was determined by the OD570 of the migrated cells stained with crystal violet, and the value from control group was arbitrarily set at 100%. As plotted in Fig. 6(B), 42.74% decrease in cell migration rate was tested in Cur–LPUS–MBs group, which was much lower than other groups (p <0.01).

It is well established that cytoskeleton elements were closely related to maintain cell morphology and motility. The structural changes of F-actin microfilaments in MDA-MB-231 cells were observed through staining by FITC-labeled phalloidin, and DAPI was used to label nuclei. The staining of untreated cells showed a mainly regular cytoplasmic arrangement of F-actin (Fig. 7 a), and the cells treated by Cur changed slightly (Fig. 7b). However, cells treated by LPUS, LPUS–MBs and Cur–LPUS presented a certain disorder of F-actin microfilaments, showing highlight green fluorescent spots (Fig. 7c–e). Especially, the F-actin in cells treated by Cur–LPUS–MBs aggregated obviously and formed more dot-like structures, demonstrating an absolute damage of F-actin network and complete disruption of actin microfilaments (Fig. 7f).

The cavitation level was assessed by HTA production in the reaction medium immediately after sonication. At the maximum excitation wavelength of 310nm, the fluorescence emission spectrum of HTA was scanned from 350nm to 550nm by spectrofluorimetry. Fig. 8 verified that a marked increase of fluorescence intensity of HTA was observed in LPUS–MBs group compared to either the untreated control or the LPUS alone treatment groups. MBs could positively strengthen the acoustic cavitation under the experimental condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LPUS–MBs enhanced membrane permeability of MDA-MB-231 cells
ce:section.ce:para: PI staining combined with flow cytometry was used to evaluate the changes of cell membrane permeability. The level of intracellular fluorescence intensity of PI is proportional to the loss of cell membrane integrity. Results in Fig. 9 revealed that LPUS increased 14.23% cells with high PI fluorescence (p <0.05 versus control). Apparently, the application of MBs promoted the role of LPUS, the proportion of cells with high PI fluorescence was up to 30.17%. Accordingly, the fluorescence intensity of PI in cells treated by LPUS–MBs were the much higher than that of other groups as shown in the histogram.

As shown in results of flow cytometry (Fig. 10 ), in the same amount of cells counted (10,000cells), the fluorescence intensity of intracellular Cur was much higher in the cells insonated with LPUS–MBs than that treated with Cur or Cur–LPUS (p <0.01). On the other hand, the relative quantitative analysis of Cur-uptake in MDA-MB-231 cells was performed by fluorescence spectrophotometry. Fig. 11 (A) inset showed that the fluorescent excitation peak of Cur observed at 422.5nm, and the fluorescent emission peak at 549nm. By measuring the fluorescent emission intensity of Cur, the linear plot for the variation of Cur (0–20μM) was made as shown in Fig. 11(A). On this basis, we then analyzed the cellular content of Cur in MDA-MB-231 cells after different treatment. Compared with free Cur or Cur–LPUS groups, the cellular Cur concentration was significantly higher in the Cur–LPUS–MBs group (Fig. 11(B)). These data demonstrated MBs-assisted LPUS could significantly promote Cur transport into MDA-MB-231 cells, to contribute it play a stronger cytotoxicity.

Among the various therapeutic applications, UMTD has drawn much research attention. This approach is a safe and noninvasive approach to enhance the delivery of therapeutic agents to the lesion tissues, due to its advantages of low immunogenicity and toxicity, repeatability and regional tissue-targeted specificity. Concisely, UMTD uses ultrasound and gas-encapsulated MBs to induce openings on a nearby surface, allowing for increased permeability across the cell membrane or vessel wall, leading to successful drug delivery [13–15,26–27].

It is generally believed that the sonoporation process is highly associated with the energetic cavitation activities (volumetric expansion, contraction, fragmentation, and collapse) of the MBs, which is closely related to the experimental parameters [14,26]. In the present study, we chose the LPUS as sonication approach at a frequency of 870kHz, an intensity of 1W/cm2, duration of 90s, and its operating mode is 5s on and 2s off. The terephthalate dosimetry experiment verified that MBs significantly improved the level of acoustic cavitation induced by LPUS (Fig. 8). Accordingly, the results of PI uptake assay suggested that in presence of MBs, LPUS significantly affect the plasma membrane permeability as compared to either the control or LPUS-alone group (Fig. 9). Despite some damage of cell morphology induced by LPUS or LPUS–MBs treatment (Figs. 3 and 4), the cell survival rate was over 80% (Fig. 2). Based on these results, we speculated that the plasma membrane permeability of MDA-MB-231 cells may be markedly improved by LPUS–MBs treatment under the experimental condition in this study, which would help drugs transport into cells but not powerful to kill MDA-MB-231 cells.

Cur has been proved to be a good candidate for treating cancerous diseases. In spite of its efficacy and safety, Cur has not yet been approved as an officially therapeutic agent for cancer, and its restricted bioavailability has been highlighted as a major problem for this [20,28–29]. As is known, one of the criteria that determine the degree of malignant cell viability inhibition suppressed by Cur is dependent on its intracellular content. In present study, data in Figs. 10 and 11 showed that the cellular uptake of Cur was markedly promoted by LPUS application in the presence of MBs when compared with free Cur group. Therefore, we inferred that the enhanced cellular membrane permeability and accompanying increase in Cur-uptake were triggered by the cavitation effects induced by LPUS–MBs, which would significantly promote pharmacological effect of Cur. As expected, coupled with LPUS–MBs, cytotoxicity of Cur was greatly facilitated on MDA-MB-231 cells (Figs. 2–4); especially the proportion of apoptosis significantly higher than other groups (Fig. 5).

It was worth noting that, cell morphology and motility were obviously damaged caused by Cur combined with LPUS–MBs (Figs. 3, 4 and 6), suggesting that the actin cytoskeleton homeostasis would be affected because the actin cytoskeleton is accountable for a variety of cell physiological events, including metastasis, motility and apoptosis [30]. To prove this point, we observed the changes of F-actin cytoskeleton in MDA-MB-231 cells post treatment. As shown in Fig. 7, the disordered arrangement of F-actin and complete disappearance of microfilament network was observed in cells treated by Cur–LPUS–MBs. This outcome certified that Cur combined with LPUS–MBs could seriously disrupt the F-actin cytoskeleton of MDA-MB-231 cells.

It is incredibly valuable to explore approaches enhancing pharmacological effects of Cur as its tremendous potential in the aspect of anti-tumor therapy. We believe that UMTD may be a potential efficient way to promote intracellular accumulation of Cur, leading to more remarkable therapeutic effect, as evidenced by results in present study. However, the therapeutic effectiveness and special mechanisms of Cur with UMTD still need to be clarified further. On the other hand, one of important aspects of UMTD induced membrane permeabilization under discussion, is the duration of increased cell membrane permeability. The actual transient process of sonoporation and the subsequent very fast resealing of cell membrane perforations would affect the process of UMTD [8,31]. Researchers now credit that sonoporation will effectively promote drug uptake by cell if the drug molecules are physically located in the vicinity of MBs subjected to ultrasound and cells. As these effects will only occur where and when ultrasound is applied, drug delivery is expected only to happen in the insonated part [32–34]. Preliminary data revealed that Cur combined with clinical SonoVue® and therapeutic LPUS was more effective in cell viability inhibition compared with the sole use of either Cur or LPUS–MBs; and importantly we confirmed the destruction of cytoskeletal structures and apoptosis induction could involve in this process. Many factors associated with this phenomenon play an important role. Additionally, it is an urgent problem to be solved that how to effectively utilize UMTD to promote Cur accumulation in the local lesions. We will further study in depth to provide a basis for future clinical combined application of Cur and ultrasound.

In summary, we demonstrated the anti-tumor efficacy of Cur administration combined with therapeutic LPUS and clinical SonoVue® MBs on breast cancer MDA-MB-231 cells. The combined treatment showed remarkable cytotoxicity on cells, revealing significant apoptosis induction, F-actin cytoskeleton disruption and cell motility inhibition. These bio-effects could be attributed to the enhanced ultrasonic cavitation, increased membrane permeabilization and subsequent cellular Cur uptake. The results illustrated that combination of MBs and LPUS would be a potential strategy to promote cellular uptake of Cur, decline the damage threshold of Cur and improve its anti-tumor efficiency.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81472846), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (GK201502009, GK201504011), and National College students’ innovative program (201510718011).
